title: Decision Science: Policy Design and Clinical Decision-Making Through Health Economics
text:
On April 19, 2025, I presented this talk on Decision Science at the Boston Japanese Researchers Forum.
The talk walks through three parts: what Decision Science is and the eight-step process used to analyze decisions, a hands-on example applying that process to a simple everyday choice, and real-world use cases showing how this approach has shaped national screening guidelines, vaccine recommendations, and drug pricing.
Decision Science integrates economics, statistics, and behavioral science to support choices under uncertainty by explicitly weighing effects, costs, and the risk involved. Rather than relying on intuition alone, it builds simulation models including decision trees, Markov models, and others, which estimate outcomes like QALYs (quality-adjusted life years) and compare options using cost-effectiveness analysis. I worked through a flight-seat-class example step by step, including an audience exercise to elicit utility values, then showed how the same logic underlies decisions made by the USPSTF, the CDC’s ACIP, and Japan’s drug-pricing system. I closed by discussing where this approach runs into real limits when assumptions drive results, when utility is hard to measure, and when economic efficiency is not the right yardstick at all.
Download PDF file.

title: Satoshi Koiso/小磯 聡

English日本語


I am a PhD student in Health Economics and Outcomes Research at the Comparative Health Outcomes, Policy, and Economics (CHOICE) Institute at the University of Washington. My research focuses on health decision science and economic evaluation, with the goal of quantifying the value of policies and translating uncertainty into actionable guidance for policymakers, particularly on behalf of populations whose voices are often absent from the policymaking process.
My area of interest includes: 

Economic evaluation for underrepresented populations worldwide, through the use of simulation modeling to estimate the potential effects of policies and clinical measures where empirical evidence is limited and decisions have historically been made anecdotally rather than through rigorous, evidence-based analysis.
Methodological advancement for emerging public health threats, including new health economic approaches suited to high-uncertainty, rapidly evolving challenges, such as future pandemics, climate change, and artificial intelligence, where conventional methods struggle to keep pace.

In the future, I aspire to two intertwined roles:

As a policy advisor, I aim to bring rigorous methods to bear on real decision-making problems, translating complex evidence into clear, actionable guidance at the table where decisions about population health and clinical practice are actually made.
As an educator, I hope to share not only technical skills but the lived experience of navigating research across disciplines, institutions, and cultures, nurturing the next generation of scholars and co-creating knowledge with them rather than simply transmitting it.

I am a strong believer in the power of diversity, equity, and inclusion, and have supported this commitment outside my professional work, including serving as a scientific committee member of the R-HTA in Low- and Middle-Income Countries chapter, and completing a solo cycling fundraising ride along Route 66 to support family and community health efforts in Nigeria. My aspiration is to help build a world where everyone, regardless of where they live or how readily their voice is currently heard, can pursue their fullest, healthiest life.



Research Intern, Health Division, Directorate for Employment, Labour, and Social Affairs, Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD), Paris, France (Jun-Aug 2026)
Research Specialist, Medical Practice Evaluation Center, Massachusetts General Hospital, Boston, MA (Jul 2022-Jun 2025)

Master of Development Practice, Goldman School of Public Policy, University of California, Berkeley, Berkeley, CA (May 2022)
Bachelor of Veterinary Science (DVM equivalent), The University of Tokyo, Tokyo, Japan (Mar 2016)




米国シアトルのワシントン大学CHOICE Institute（Comparative Health Outcomes, Policy, and Economics）にて、医療経済・アウトカム研究（Health Economics and Outcomes Research）の博士課程に在籍しています。医療における意思決定科学と経済評価を専門とし、不確実な状況下における医療政策の経済的価値を定量化することで、政策立案者に意思決定の指針を示すことを目指しています。特に、政策立案の現場で、声が届きにくい社会的弱者の視点を届けることを大切にしています。
関心のあるテーマは以下の通りです。

あらゆる国において、社会的弱者を対象とした経済評価。実証的なエビデンスが乏しく、これまで経験則や政治的圧力に基づいて意思決定がなされてきた政策・臨床上の施策について、数理・統計モデルを用いてその医療効果・経済効果を推計します。
新興の公衆衛生上の脅威に対応するための研究手法の開発。将来のパンデミック、気候変動、人工知能など、不確実性が高く急速に変化する課題など、従来の手法では対応が難しい領域に適した新たな医療経済学的アプローチを構築しています。

将来的には、以下の二つの役割を担いたいと考えています。

政策アドバイザーとして、厳密な研究手法を実際の意思決定の現場に活かし、複雑なエビデンスを、人々の健康や臨床に関する意思決定が実際になされる場において、明確で実践的な指針へと昇華すること。
教育者として、技術的なスキルだけでなく、分野・組織・文化を横断して研究に取り組んできた自らの経験を伝え、次世代の研究者を育みながら、一方的に知識を伝えるのではなく共に知識を作り上げていくこと。

多様性・公平性・包摂性（DEI）の力を強く信じており、本業以外でもその実践に取り組んでいます。R-HTA in Low- and Middle-Income Countries chapter の運営委員を務めているほか、ナイジェリアの家族・地域医療の支援のため、ルート66を単独で自転車横断するチャリティーライド を行いました。どこに暮らしていても、また声が政策立案に届きやすい立場にあるかどうかにかかわらず、誰もが自分らしく人生を全うできる世界の実現に貢献したいと考えています。

title: Pandemic Response in Low- and Middle-Income Countries
text:
Abstract
The COVID-19 pandemic exposed and exacerbated pre-existing inequities in health systems worldwide, disproportionately affecting people with disabilities. In low- and middle-income countries (LMICs), these challenges were intensified by limited resources, underdeveloped or weakly enforced legal protections, fragile health infrastructure, and pervasive social stigma. As a result, people with disabilities frequently encountered barriers to timely information, preventive measures, healthcare services, and economic stability. These problems were compounded by a lack of political representation, scarcity of disability-disaggregated data, and the competing priorities that characterize policymaking during emergencies. Current advocacy for disability inclusion in pandemics often relies on humanitarian or human-rights rhetoric. While morally compelling, such arguments may have limited influences on policymaking in emergency contexts, where policymakers must prioritize among multiple urgent demands. This entry, therefore, advances pragmatic arguments for disability-inclusive pandemic responses, emphasizing their potential public health advantages, such as reducing severe infections, secondary transmissions, and outbreaks in care facilities, and economic advantages, including lower long-term healthcare costs and improved workforce participation. Organizations of Persons with Disabilities (OPDs) emerge as critical actors in bridging the gap between people with disabilities and policymakers. By gathering first-hand data, articulating their unmet needs, and demonstrating the broader benefits of inclusive strategies, OPDs can help integrate disability considerations into emergency public health planning. Strengthening disability-inclusive approaches is essential not only for safeguarding the rights of people with disabilities but also for enhancing the equity, resilience, and efficiency of health systems in LMICs in the face of future pandemics and public health emergencies.

title: Pandemic Response in Low- and Middle-Income Countries

title: Japan’s Health Insurance System and Reimbursement for AI Medical Devices
text:
On April 29, 2026, I presented this talk on Japan’s health insurance system and reimbursement for AI medical devices at the CHOICE Graduate Seminar, University of Washington.
The talk walks through three parts: an overview of how Japan’s public health insurance system is structured and financed, how medical services, drugs, and devices are approved and priced under the national fee schedule, and how AI medical devices fit or struggle to fit into that existing framework.
Japan provides near-universal health coverage, but an aging population and shrinking workforce are putting growing pressure on its sustainability. AI medical devices are emerging as a potential tool to ease labor shortages in healthcare, but their intangible and evolving nature makes them hard to evaluate under a system built for fixed-price drugs and devices. Drawing on two real use cases, I discussed how regulators and payers are currently improvising solutions, and where the open questions remain.
Download PDF file.
